response:
To find the maximum value of $ z = 2x + 3y $ given the system of inequalities  
$$
\begin{cases}
x + y - 1 \geq 0 \\
y - x - 1 \leq 0 \\
x \leq 1
\end{cases}
$$  
we need to first understand the feasible region defined by these constraints. This region is a set of points $ (x, y) $ that satisfy all three inequalities simultaneously. The finding of this region involves analyzing the intersection of the half-planes defined by each inequality.

The first inequality $ x + y - 1 \geq 0 $ can be rewritten as $ x + y \geq 1 $. This represents the area above the line $ x + y = 1 $. The second inequality $ y - x - 1 \leq 0 $ becomes $ y - x \leq 1 $ or $ y \leq x + 1 $. This represents the area below the line $ y = x + 1 $. The third inequality $ x \leq 1 $ restricts the values of $ x $ to be less than or equal to 1.

Finding the intersection of these three regions gives a polygonal feasible region. To find the maximum value of $ z = 2x + 3y $ within this region we need to evaluate $ z $ at the vertices of the feasible region. The vertices are found by solving the system of equations formed by the boundary lines of the inequalities.

The lines $ x + y = 1 $ and $ y = x + 1 $ intersect when we solve  
$$
x + (x + 1) = 1 \Rightarrow 2x + 1 = 1 \Rightarrow x = 0 \Rightarrow y = 1
$$  
So one vertex is $ (0, 1) $.

The line $ x + y = 1 $ and $ x = 1 $ intersect when  
$$
1 + y = 1 \Rightarrow y = 0
$$  
So another vertex is $ (1, 0) $.

The line $ y = x + 1 $ and $ x = 1 $ intersect when  
$$
y = 1 + 1 = 2
$$  
So the third vertex is $ (1, 2) $.

Now evaluating $ z = 2x + 3y $ at these vertices
